title: Progress for the Poor By Lane Kenworthy Oxford University Press. 2011. 168 pages. $75 cloth
abstract: Progress for the Poor is a short reference book assessing the progress in reducing absolute poverty across 17 affluent countries during the past three-and-half decades. Using data from a variety of sources including the Luxembourg Income Study and Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development, it shows that almost all of these countries have witnessed improvements in household incomes after taxes and transfer which helped reduce absolute poverty during 1979 and 2007. Lane Kenworthy, the author of the book, argues, however, that this encouraging picture may have resulted from government transfers more than from trickling down of economic growth itself. While households typically realize the fruits of growth through increased employment and earnings, the conclusion here is that those at the bottom of the distribution cannot automatically benefit unless the government steps up its redistributive efforts. This conclusion in and of itself is not new since this has been discussed widely by policy researchers, sociologists, economists, and even political scientists.
